coredata.dc:title: A multi-responsive regenerable water-stable two-dimensional cadmium (II) fluorescent probe for highly selective, sensitive and real-time sensing of nitrofurazone and cupric ion
coredata.dc:creator: An, Jun Dan
Wang, Tian Tian
Shi, Yang Fan
Wu, Xiang Xia
Liu, Yuan Yuan
Huo, Jian Zhong
Ding, Bin
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, luminescent metal organic frameworks (MOFs) materials have attracted widespread attention in the field of fluorescence sensing. In this work, a novel two-dimensional (2D) micro-porous water-stable coordination material {[Cd(μ3-HL)2(H2O)]⋅2.3H2O}n (1, H2L = 4,4′-(1H-1,2,4-triazol-1-yl)methylene-bis (benzonic acid)) has been prepared through the powerful solvo-thermal approaches, which has been characterized by single crystal X-ray diffraction analysis, PXRD, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization. Crystal structural analysis also reveals the partly de-pronated μ3-coordinated HL− adopted tri-dentate bridging mode, which link these cadmium (II) centers, eventually forming the two-dimensional (2D) micro-porous coordination framework of 1. 1D channel with diemsionality of 12.216(3) Å × 8.578(11) Å that can be observed along the crystallographic a axis. It is noted that 1 has good chemical stability and maintains structural integrity not only in various solvents, but also in aqueous solutions with pH values varying from 2 to 12. Additionally, 1 has the unique advantages such as high detection sensitivity for Nitrofurazone (NZF) and Cupric ion (Cu2+) (Ksv quenching efficiency for NZF: 5.0 × 106 M−1 and Cu2+ 5.6 × 106 M−1) and low detection limits (NZF: 1.21 μM and Cu2+: 1.07 μM), good selectivity and good real-time performance. The result of photo-luminescent experiment demonstrates that 1 can realize high sensitivity and real-time chemical discrimination of antibiotics and ions, and has good reusability. To the best of our knowledge, 1 is the first example of the ultra-stable 2D cadmium (II) micro-porous coordination material as a renewable chemical sensor for NZF and Cu2+.

head.ce:title: A multi-responsive regenerable water-stable two-dimensional cadmium (II) fluorescent probe for highly selective, sensitive and real-time sensing of nitrofurazone and cupric ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Inorganic-Organic Hybrid Functional Material Chemistry (Tianjin Normal University), Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratoryof Structure and Performance for Functional Molecule, College of Chemistry, Tianjin Normal University, 393 Binshui West Road, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, luminescent metal organic frameworks (MOFs) materials have attracted widespread attention in the field of fluorescence sensing. In this work, a novel two-dimensional (2D) micro-porous water-stable coordination material {[Cd(μ3-HL)2(H2O)]⋅2.3H2O}n (1, H2L = 4,4′-(1H-1,2,4-triazol-1-yl)methylene-bis (benzonic acid)) has been prepared through the powerful solvo-thermal approaches, which has been characterized by single crystal X-ray diffraction analysis, PXRD, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization. Crystal structural analysis also reveals the partly de-pronated μ3-coordinated HL− adopted tri-dentate bridging mode, which link these cadmium (II) centers, eventually forming the two-dimensional (2D) micro-porous coordination framework of 1. 1D channel with diemsionality of 12.216(3) Å × 8.578(11) Å that can be observed along the crystallographic a axis. It is noted that 1 has good chemical stability and maintains structural integrity not only in various solvents, but also in aqueous solutions with pH values varying from 2 to 12. Additionally, 1 has the unique advantages such as high detection sensitivity for Nitrofurazone (NZF) and Cupric ion (Cu2+) (Ksv quenching efficiency for NZF: 5.0 × 106 M−1 and Cu2+ 5.6 × 106 M−1) and low detection limits (NZF: 1.21 μM and Cu2+: 1.07 μM), good selectivity and good real-time performance. The result of photo-luminescent experiment demonstrates that 1 can realize high sensitivity and real-time chemical discrimination of antibiotics and ions, and has good reusability. To the best of our knowledge, 1 is the first example of the ultra-stable 2D cadmium (II) micro-porous coordination material as a renewable chemical sensor for NZF and Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, micro-porous coordination polymers or metal organic frameworks have received great attention because of their unique properties and applications [1–3]. Luminescent coordination polymers (LCPs) have attracted more and more attention in recent years because these materials possess unique advantages such as adjustable fluorescence intensity, high sensitivity, diversity of motif, variable topological structure, simple synthetic method and tunable porosity [4–12]. Fluorescence technology is interesting and favorable because the detection method has high sensitivity, quick response, simple operation and low cost [13–16]. It has opened up a new prospect for the detection of different cations, anions, organic small molecules, biological molecules, genetics and even biological metabolism activities by LCPs. These applications are of great significance for qualitative and quantitative analysis and control of chemical substances, so as to further understand the life processes of genes and cells [17].

At present, antibiotics have been extensively used to treat bacterial infection in human and animals. However, when these antibiotics are overused, they will turn into a kind of organic contaminants in the aqueous environment. Therefore, considering that overuse of antibiotics may cause harm to human health and environmental protection, it is of great significance to quickly identify antibiotics in water environment [18]. On the other hand, cupric(II) ion (Cu2+) is a trace mineral, which is essential to the human body. It can be diffused into the blood in 15 min after it exists in and out of red blood cells at the same time. It is helpful for iron to transfer into protein, which plays a key catalytic role in the formation of heme. Unfortunately, the lack of Cu2+ in the body can lead to anemia, hair abnormalities, brain disorders, bone abnormalities, while excess Cu2+ can lead to vomiting, diarrhea, cirrhosis, dyskinesia and perceptual neurological disorders. Therefore, it has practical significance to design a discriminating platform to detect antibiotics NZF and Cu2+, so as to ensure a good chemical reaction process and protect the homeostasis of the biological environment.

Currently, 1,2,4-triazoles and poly-carboxylic acid derivatives are attractive because these building blocks have numerous different bridging modes, which can bridge central metal ions/metal clusters and construct functional coordination polymers with intriguing properties. Further, people have paid more attention to the formation of functional coordination materials through utilizing the bi-functional ligands simultaneously containing the building blocks of 1,2,4-triazoles and poly-carboxylic acid. As shown in Scheme 1 , the bi-functional multi-dentate ligand (4,4′-(1H-1,2,4-triazol-1-yl)methylene-bis(benzonic acid)) (H2L) can be adopted, considering the following three aspects: (1) Firstly, H2L contains one triazole and two carboxylate moieties, which can provide numerous coordinate sites to bridge central metal ions/metal clusters; (2) Secondly, nitrogen and oxygen atoms from H2L can provide additional supra-molecular interactions to consolidate the coordination structure through hydrogen bond interactions; (3) Thirdly, because H2L possesses numerous π electrons, the combination of bi-phenyl rings, triazole and carboxylate moieties not only can generate stable and rigid skeletons, but also provide wide conjugation moieties to construct these coordination polymers with excellent fluorescent performance. All of these factors are conducive to the generation of strong fluorescence emissions, therefore H2L is expected to be utilized as an ideal component for these fluorescent coordination polymers.

In this work, a novel water-stable coordination material {[Cd(μ3-HL)2(H2O)]⋅2.3H2O}n (1) (H2L = 4,4′-(1H-1,2,4-triazol-1-yl)methylene-bis(benzonic acid)) has been prepared through the solvo-thermal method. The solvo-thermal synthesis provides a quick and easy preparation method for the water-stable coordination material 1. As the fluorescent coordination polymer, 1 has some advantages because 1 is water stable and has high KSV value in discriminating antibiotics NZF (Ksv quenching efficiency is 5.0 × 106 M−1 and low detection limit 1.21 μM). On the other hand, it has high detection sensitivity, good selectivity and good real-time performance for Cu2+ (Ksv quenching efficiency is 5.6 × 106 M−1 and low detection limit 1.07 μM). At the same time, we performed PXRD analysis on large-volume samples prepared from coordination polymer 1, and the results showed that the prepared large-volume samples had higher purity. 1 behaves good chemical stability and can maintain its structure framework in a variety of solvents and pH. Photoluminescence recoverability experiments also show that 1 has a good cycle in the detection of the antibiotics NZF and Cu2+. In addition, we have also performed the fluorescence lifetime experiment before and after the detection process to discuss the discrimination mechanism. In conclusion, 1 can be used as an excellent bi-functional photoluminescence sensor for NZF and Cu2+ [19,20].

In this work, 4,4′-(1H-1,2,4-triazol-1-yl)methylene-bis(benzonic acid) (H2L) was prepared by alkaline hydrolysis of 4,4’-((1H-1,2,4-triazol-1-yl)methylene) dibenzonitrile, followed by acidification (Scheme S1) [21]. The 1H NMR spectrum of H2L in DMSO‑d 6 also is also made (Fig. S1), 1HNMR (400Hz, DMSO‑d 6): δ8.68 (s, 3H), 8.11 (s, 3H), 7.95 (d, J = 8Hz, 11H), 7.87 (d, J = 8Hz, 1H), 7.32(dd, J1 = 8Hz, J2 = 16Hz, 15H), 3.39(s, xH), 2.37(m, xH), 1.79 (d, 3H). All the other chemical reagents were analytical grade and were not further purified. Furazolidone (FZD), Nitrofurantoin (NFT), Metronidazole (MDZ), Nitrofurazone (NZF), Ronidazole (RNZ), Dimetridazole (DMZ), Ornidazole (ORN), Chloramphenicol (CHL), Sulfadiazine (SDZ), Thiamphenicol (THI) and Sulfamethazine (SMZ) were purchased from Tianjin Kaimat Chemical Technology Company. CuCl2·2H2O, CdCl2, ZnCl2, MnCl2·4H2O, NaCl, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, MgCl2, Ca(NO3)2, FeCl2, Sr(NO3)2, AlCl3 were purchased from Aladdin reagent. Ethanol was purchased from Tianjin Kangkede Technology Company. Dimethylacetamide was purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Microanalysis of Carbon, Hydrogen, and Nitrogen were performed by using the Perkin-Elmer 240 elemental analyzer. Poweder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) is performed by utilizing the D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Cu-Kα radiation). FT-IR spectra (4000-5000 cm−1) were determined to utilize the NICOLET 6700 FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets (NICOLET, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded by utilizing the PerkinElmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence discriminating experiment was recorded by utilizing the Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies), which is equipped with a plotter unit and a quartz cell (1 cm × 1 cm) in phosphorescence mode. The Nova-Nano 230 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Aircraft) was used for gold plating.

A mixture of CdCl2(32.3 mg,0.1 mmol) and H 2 L (18.3 mg 0.3 mmol) was added into deionized water (4 mL) and DMA(1 mL). The mixture was then added into a Teflon container and placed into the steel autoclave. Reaction temperature is programmatic controlled, which is heated to 90 °C for 0.5 h and kept at 90 °C for 48 h, then is cooled to room temperature during 24 h. The resulting colorless lumpy single crystals can be washed with water and ether several times. Elemental analysis for C34H30.6CdN6O11.3: Calcd (%): C, 50.02; H, 3.77; N, 10.29; Found (%): C, 50.59; H, 3.89; N, 10.57. FT-IR (cm−1, KBr): 3447(br), 3112(m), 1710(s), 1610(w), 1591(m), 1527(s), 1406(s), 1181(m), 1141(w), 1115(w), 1004(w), 852(w), 761(s), 743(m). These as-synthesized samples need to be pre-treated before FT-IR and other characterization.

The diffraction intensity of 1 is collected by utilizing the intelligent 1000 CCD diffractometer (graphite-monochromated Mo-Kα radiation, λ = 0.71073 Å). The ω-ϕ scanning strategy is applied. Lorentz polarization and absorption correction are applied. SHELXS-97 and SHELXL-97 are utilized to refine the structure, and the direct method is utilized to solve and use the full matrix least squares technique for refinement. All the non-hydrogen atoms have anisotropic thermal parameters. The geometric generation of organic hydrogen atoms; the hydrogen atoms of water molecules are distributed on different maps and refined by isotropic temperature factors. Introducing anomalous dispersion correction using the analytical expression of the medium atom scattering factor [22]. Guest molecules can be determined through Elemental analysis and TGA characterization. Crystallographic data and refinement details of 1 are shown in Table 1 . The table of key length and key angle as shown in Table S1, and selected hydrogen bonds lengths [Å] and angles [°] calculated by PLATON program were listed in Table S2. CCDC-1848542 represents the crystal data for this work. This data is freely available through the Cambridge Crystal Data Center.

Coordination polymer 1 crystallizes in the monoclinic crystal system with P21/n space group. As depicted in Fig. 1 , the basic structural unit of 1 has one Cd(II) center (Cd1), two partly de-pronated HL−, one terminal coordination water molecule (O9) and 2.3 lattice water molecules. Cd1 is seven-coordinated by two triazole nitrogen atom (N3A, N6), four carboxylate oxygen atoms (O1, O2, O7, O8) and one terminal O9 water molecule forming the bi-pyramidal coordination geometry. The Cd-N bond lengths range from 2.285(5) to 2.371(4) Å, and Cd-O bond lengths range from 2.279(4) to 2.562(4) Å, which all fall into the normal range and can be compared with previous reported bond results [23]. As for the partly de-pronated HL−, its carboxylate oxygen atoms (O1 and O2; O7 and O8) and triazole nitrogen atoms (N3A, N6) are involved in the μ3-coordination mode, while the other carboxylate oxygen atoms (O3 and O4; O5 and O6) are not de-pronated and not coordinated by the CdII centers.

These partly de-pronated HL− further link neighboring CdII centers, which construct the two-dimensional micro-porous network of 1 (Fig. 2 (a)). 1D micro-porous channel with diemsionality of 12.216(3) Å × 8.578(11) Å can be found along the crystallographic a axis. It is noted that some non-classical hydrogen bonds also exist between two oxygen atoms (O3, O6 and O9) and these carbon atoms (C16, C17, C19 and C30), as it is indicated by numerous hydrogen bonds (O(3)-H(3)···O(7) (2.6763(7)Å), O(6)-H(6)···O(2) (2.6581(6)Å), O(9)-H(9B)···O(8)(2.7715(7)Å), C(16)-H(16)···O(4)(3.3033(8)Å), C(17)-H(17)···O(5) (3.0982(8) Å) and C(19)-H(19)···O(8) (3.2539(8) Å), C(30)-H(30)···O(3) (3.4381(8) Å)). These weak interactions also play a vital role in stabilizing and extending the two-dimensional micro-porous structure into the three-dimensional supra-molecular architecture (Fig. 2(b)) [24,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PXRD, TGA, SEM, TEM, FT-IR and photoluminescence characterization for 1
ce:section.ce:para: Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern determination of the as-synthesized coordination material 1 was carried out (Fig. 3 ). Experimental results agree well with theoretical calculations [26–29]. These PXRD peaks are well consistent with theoretical PXRD patterns position and the sharpness of the lines. Experimental results also show that the as-synthesized 1 is in good agreement with the crystal structure. Some little changes in diffraction intensity between experimental and theoretical diagrams can be related to different crystal orientations of these materials [30–33].

UV–vis spectra of H 2 L and 1 in aqueous solution were also investigated, showing that H 2 L and 1 have similar typical bands (Fig. S2). The photoluminescence emissions of 1 in the solid-state state have been performed at ambient temperature. It also reveals that 1 exhibited strong luminescence at 418 nm under excitation at 307 nm (Fig. 4 (a)), which can be ascribed to large conjugated bi-phenyl backbones [34–36]. In order to investigate the thermal stability of 1, the thermal decomposition process of 1 in the solid-state is also determined. As shown in Fig. 4(b), 1 shows two subsequent weight loss: First, the first weight loss is from 25 °C to 110 °C, and a weight loss of 7.3%, which can correspond to the loss of one coordinated water and 2.3 lattice water molecules (the theoretical weight loss is 6.7%); and the subsequent weight loss is followed by 110 °C–600 °C. The second weight loss is 83.9%, which can correspond to the loss of the backbone decomposition of coordination complex 1. Finally, 1 was completely decomposed into the corresponding oxidized CdO, and the remaining experiment weight was 16.1% and close to theoretical remaining weight 15.73%.

Solvent stability and pH stability of coordination material 1 were determined, and the powder samples of 1 were added to different solvent solutions (such as cyclohexane, acetone, chloroform, ethanol, n-hexane, isopropanol and water), respectively, then the resulting solutions are suspended for 24 h. The resulting solutions were filtered and then the solid material is dried in a vacuum furnace. Then PXRD was used to characterize the dried materials [51–54]. As exhibited in Fig. 7 , PXRD patterns of suspended 1 in aqueous solutions with different pH values and in different solvents, they are still consistent with the simulated model based on crystal data, which indicates that coordination network 1 can remain unchanged and is stable in acidic, alkaline aqueous solutions and various solvents [55–58].

Currently, antibiotics have been extensively used to treat human and animal bacterial infections. However, if these antibiotic-containing drugs are overused, it can turn into a type of organic pollutant in the aqueous environment. Therefore, considering that the excessive use of antibiotics may lead great harm to environmental protection and human health, it is of great significance to quickly identify antibiotics in the water environment [59].

Considering that the coordination material 1 has good photoluminescence properties and micro-porosity, it makes sense to use 1 as potential photo-luminescent sensors to discriminate different antibiotics such as Ronidazole (RNZ), Dimetridazole (DMZ), Nitrofurazone (NZF), Furazolidone (FZD), Nitrofurantoin (NFT), Ornidazole (ORN), Sulfadiazine (SDZ), Sulfamethazine (SMZ), Thiamphenicol (THI), Metronidazole (MDZ) and Chloramphenicol (CHL). In order to determine the discrimination ability of 1 for these micro antibiotics, the photoluminescence experiment was carried out by injecting antibiotics into the aqueous suspension of coordination polymer 1. In the titration experiments, the sample of 1 was added to a 3 mL aqueous solution, and an antibiotic was selected to form an aqueous solution containing a different antibiotic (1.8 mM). Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence quenching rate under different antibiotic dosage at room temperature. Obviously, the sensing experiment showed that coordination material 1 had a high photoluminescence quenching after adding different antibiotics, and the quenching efficiency was in the order of NZF>NFT>DMZ>ORN>MDZ>CHL>RNZ >SMZ>SDZ>THI>FZD for 1.

The experimental results show that 1 can sensitively identify Nitrofurazone (NZF) in the water environment. It can be observed that the fluorescent band of 1 is also slightly red-shifted indicating there should exist some interactions between 1 and NZF. Further, after four consecutive cycles, the luminescent intensity of the regenerated 1 still remained at 75.93%, and the luminous intensity and quenching efficiency of the initial MOF 1 remained basically unchanged (Fig. 11 (a)) [61–66]. In addition, a recyclable experiment of photoluminescence sensors was also performed. A photo-luminescent attenuation curve before and after the detection of NZF was studied, as shown in Fig. 11(b) and Table 2
, it can be observed that the luminescent lifetime of 1 is changed from 6.078 ns to 4.24 ns indicating that the resonance energy transfer between 1 and NZF. On the other hand, as demonstrated and calculated by DFT result in the previous Zr(IV)-Based Metal-Organic Frameworks, these MOFs can successfully be applied in the detection of antibiotics and organic explosives in water [67], the conduction-band (CB) energy levels of a MOF lies at higher energy level than the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of an analyte, which leads to the electron transfer from the MOF to the analyte, thus resulting in the fluorescence quenching. As a result, the co-existence of electron transfer and resonance energy transfer makes NZF show higher photo-luminescence quenching effect compared with other checked analytes. As we know, NZF is a drug that inhibits and interferes with microbial carbohydrate metabolism. This compound is used as a preservative and anti-allergic agent in some pharmaceutical formulations. Based on the comparison about detection limits between sensor 1 and related CP sensors for NZF detection (Table 4 ) [68,69], according to the above discussion, the high sensitivity, selectivity and recyclability for NZF indicate that 1 can be utilized as the excellent reproducible chemical sensor for discriminating Nitrofurazone (NZF) (see Table 3).

Copper is an indispensable micronutrient for human health. It has the key effects on the function of blood, immune system, central nervous system, liver, brain and so on. The lack of copper in the human body may result in abnormal hair, abnormal bones, arteries, anemia, and even brain disorders [72–76]. But in excess, it can lead to vomiting, dyskinesia, diarrhea, cirrhosis, and sensory neurological disorders. Therefore, it has a very important role in the discrimination of copper ions.

In order to perform the different fluorescence response of different cations, the prepared coordination materials were ground into powder samples and put into different brine solutions, which contain different cations: Cu2+,Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Sr2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Na+, Al3+. As shown in Fig. 12 , only Cu2+ shows an excellent fluorescence quenching result, while other cations have no obvious effect on the luminescence emissions of 1. PXRD patterns of 1 are also researched and it is in well consistent with the theoretical line of 1. It also shows that the structure of 1 remains unchanged in the detection process [77–79]. Thus, 1 has certain reference value for the sensitive and selective identification of Cu2+, which can be considered as the good platform for Cu2+ detection.

Furthermore, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Cu2+ plot is also investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 13 , the SV plots for Cu2+ are nearly linear varying from the concentration ranges of 0–1.8 mM. In order to quantitatively investigate the detection sensitivity, the quenching efficiency can be estimated utilizing the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation, I0/I = Ksv[A] + 1, where I and I0 are considered as the emission intensities of 1 dispersed into water solutions with and without Cu2+. [A] is considered as Cu2+ concentration, Ksv is considered as quenching coefficient. The Stern-Volmer plot for Cu2+ is typically linear at the low concentrations, which has quite high Ksv value 5.6 × 106 M −1 (R2 = 0.994). In the detection process, the PXRD pattern of 1 was determined and it was in well consistent with the simulated pattern, indicating that the structure of 1 was not invariable (Fig. 14 ).

Based on the comparison about detection limits between sensor 1 and related CP sensors for Cu2+ detection (Table 4) [70,71], the results show that 1 can effectively identify (Cu2+) in the water environment. The discriminative sensing of Cu2+ ion is not obviously affected by the addition of the other different analytes. Photo-luminescence decay curves of 1 suspension before and after the detection of Cu2+ were studied, as shown in Fig. 15 and Table 2. It can be observed that the luminescent lifetime of 1 is almost changed, indicating the state quenching mechanism. In addition, experiments on the recyclability of photoluminescence sensors are also carried out. After four consecutive cycles, the luminescence intensity recovery of 1 can remain at 80.62%, and the luminescence intensity and quenching efficiency of the initial MOF 1 remained basically unchanged, which also confirm the state quenching mechanism.

As we know, Copper ion (Cu2+) is an important basic element involved in the activity of proteins such as cytochrome oxidase and zinc-copper superoxide dismutase. Copper is also an industrial metal that plays an important role in the environment and biological systems [80]. On the basis of bioaccumulation, Cu2+ can easily accumulate in biological liver. Excessive Cu2+ accumulation can disrupt the homeostasis system and cause a range of harms, such as Wilson s and Alzheimer’s disease [81]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and selective method for the analysis of Cu2+ in environmental water. In summary, the high sensitivity, selectivity and recyclability o for Cu2+ indicate that 1 can be utilized as the excellent reproducible chemical sensor for discriminating Cu2+.

In summary, in this work the bi-functional 4,4′-(1H-1,2,4-triazol-1-yl) methylene-bis(benzonic acid) (H2L) ligand is utilized, which contains the large conjugated biphenyl aromatic backbone. Based on H2L, a unique and ultra-stable two-dimensional (2D) luminescent cadmium(II) coordination polymer {[Cd(μ3-HL)2(H2O)]⋅2.3H2O}n (1) can be isolated under the solvo-thermal conditions. These partly de-pronated μ3-coordinated HL− adopt tri-dentate bridging mode, which link these cadmium (II) centers, eventually forming the 2D coordination material 1. PXRD analysis was investigated to verify the purity of the as-synthesized sample 1. It should be noted that 1 has good chemical stability in aqueous solutions with pH 2–12 and in the various solvents. The results of photoluminescence test show that compared with other amino acid molecules, 1 can determine NZF in real time with high sensitivity and good reusability, and also Cu2+ has good reusability compared with other ions. High quenching efficiency (For NZF: 5.0 × 106 M−1 and Cu2+ 5.6 × 106 M−1) and low detection limits (NZF: 1.21 μM and Cu2+: 1.07 μM). To our knowledge, 1 is the first example of the ultra-stable 2D cadmium (II) micro-porous coordination material as a renewable chemical sensor for NZF and Cu2+. The results also show that the preparation of this unique coordination material with the bi-functional building block H2L has the broad application prospects.

Jun Dan An: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Tian Tian Wang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yang Fan Shi: Visualization, Investigation. Xiang Xia Wu: Software, Validation. Yuan Yuan Liu: Validation, Investigation. Jian Zhong Huo: Formal analysis. Bin Ding: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
